response: # Status Indicator Dots Accessibility Design Plan > **For Claude:** Use a11y-planner protocol. Review with a11y-critic after implementation. > **Compliance target:** WCAG 2.2 AA > **Users who need accessibility:** Screen reader users, keyboard-only users, low vision users, users in high contrast mode, color-blind users > **Assistive technologies:** NVDA, JAWS, VoiceOver, keyboard-only navigation **Feature:** A reusable React `StatusDot` component that communicates item status in dashboard lists, tables, sidebars, and card headers. **Risk Level:** Low **Component/Page Type:** Static status indicator / icon-as-status label --- ## Scope & Context The existing component uses green, yellow, and red dots as the only status indicator. That fails **WCAG 1.4.1 Use of Color** because users who cannot perceive or distinguish color lose the status information entirely. This plan does not introduce keyboard behavior, live regions, or focus management because the dot is not interactive and does not update as an alert. The accessibility problem is semantic communication and non-color meaning. What this plan is **not** claiming: it does not solve real-time status updates, toast notifications, alerts, or dashboard filtering. If the status changes dynamically and the user must be notified immediately, that is a separate live-region design. --- ## Semantic Structure Plan The component must expose the status meaning as text, not the visual artifact. Preferred structure: ```html Active ``` Rationale: - The colored dot is decorative once a text equivalent exists, so it should be hidden from assistive technology with `aria-hidden="true"`. - The screen-reader text communicates the semantic status: “Active,” “Warning,” “Error,” etc. - Do not use `title`. It is not a reliable accessible name mechanism across browsers and assistive technologies. - Do not use `aria-label="green dot"` because that describes appearance, not meaning. WCAG citations: - **WCAG 1.4.1 Use of Color:** color cannot be the only way status is conveyed. - **WCAG 1.1.1 Non-text Content:** the dot functions like an icon/image and needs a text alternative. - **WCAG 1.3.1 Info and Relationships:** status information must be programmatically determinable. ### Status Text Mapping Final labels must match product terminology. Proposed baseline: | Color | Semantic Status | Screen Reader Text | |---|---|---| | Green | Active / Online / Passing | `Active` | | Yellow | Warning / Pending / Degraded | `Warning` | | Red | Error / Offline / Failing | `Error` | If different contexts need different labels, the component must accept a required semantic `label` prop rather than deriving meaning only from color. Example acceptable API direction: ```text StatusDot variant="green" label="Passing" StatusDot variant="yellow" label="Degraded" StatusDot variant="red" label="Failing" ``` The color prop alone is not enough because green/yellow/red meanings vary across tasks, team members, and deployments. --- ## Interaction Pattern Design There is no APG interactive widget pattern for this component because the dot is static content, not a button, link, checkbox, tab, menu, or live alert. | Widget | APG Pattern | Keyboard Model | ARIA Attributes | WCAG Citation | |---|---|---|---|---| | Status dot visual | None: decorative visual indicator once text exists | Not focusable | `aria-hidden="true"` on the visual dot | 1.1.1, 1.4.1 | | Status label text | Native text | Not focusable | No ARIA required if visible or visually hidden correctly | 1.1.1, 1.3.1, 1.4.1 | Do not add `tabindex`, `role="button"`, `aria-live`, or keyboard handlers. That would create false interactivity. Alternative allowed pattern: `role="img" aria-label="Active"` on the dot element. This is acceptable only when there is no sibling text label. The preferred plan is sibling visually-hidden text because it works consistently in full-label and compact modes and avoids overloading a decorative shape with semantics. --- ## Focus Management Plan The status dot itself must not receive focus. - No `tabindex`. - No keyboard interaction. - No focus restoration. - No focus trap. - If the dot appears inside a focusable row, card link, table row action, or navigation item, the parent interaction keeps its existing focus behavior. - Compact mode must not remove the accessible status label from the accessibility tree. WCAG citations: - **WCAG 2.4.3 Focus Order:** static indicators should not add unnecessary stops. - **WCAG 2.1.1 Keyboard:** no keyboard-only functionality is introduced or blocked. --- ## State Communication Design | State | Visual Indicator | Programmatic Indicator | ARIA Attribute | WCAG Citation | |---|---|---|---|---| | Active / Online / Passing | Green dot | Text label: `Active`, `Online`, or `Passing` | `aria-hidden="true"` on dot; visible or sr-only text sibling | 1.1.1, 1.4.1, 1.3.1 | | Warning / Pending / Degraded | Yellow dot | Text label: `Warning`, `Pending`, or `Degraded` | `aria-hidden="true"` on dot; visible or sr-only text sibling | 1.1.1, 1.4.1, 1.3.1 | | Error / Offline / Failing | Red dot | Text label: `Error`, `Offline`, or `Failing` | `aria-hidden="true"` on dot; visible or sr-only text sibling | 1.1.1, 1.4.1, 1.3.1 | | Compact mode | Dot remains visible; text visually hidden | Same text remains in DOM via sr-only class | No `aria-hidden` on label | 1.4.1, 1.1.1 | | Disabled or unavailable item, if applicable | Muted styling plus text | Text must include unavailable state if meaningful | Prefer native disabled semantics on parent control, not dot | 4.1.2 | No live region is needed unless status changes asynchronously and the change is urgent or user-triggered. For ordinary dashboard rendering, screen reader users will encounter the status while navigating the item. --- ## Visual Accessibility Plan The dot color system may remain unchanged, but each dot must meet **WCAG 1.4.11 Non-text Contrast**: - The dot must have at least **3:1 contrast against its adjacent background**. - Test against every actual container background: sidebar, table row, selected row, hover row, card header, dark mode, and high contrast theme. - Do not test only against white unless white is the actual adjacent color. - If a dot color fails 3:1, add a non-color-preserving visual treatment such as a contrasting outline, ring, border, or shape treatment around the dot. - Color still cannot be the only status cue, even if contrast passes. Compact mode: - Text labels may be visually hidden with a standard `.sr-only` / visually-hidden utility. - Do not use `display: none`, `visibility: hidden`, or `aria-hidden="true"` on the label. - The hidden text must remain available to NVDA, JAWS, and VoiceOver. High contrast mode: - Verify that Windows High Contrast / forced colors still conveys status meaning. - If forced colors collapse custom dot colors, the accessible text still preserves meaning. - Consider a visible shape or outline variant if design allows, but the minimum requirement is preserved semantic text. WCAG citations: - **WCAG 1.4.1 Use of Color** - **WCAG 1.4.11 Non-text Contrast** - **WCAG 1.4.3 Contrast Minimum** for any visible text labels - **WCAG 1.4.10 Reflow** if compact mode appears at narrow widths --- ## Content Accessibility Plan Status labels must describe meaning, not color. Good labels: - `Active` - `Online` - `Passing` - `Warning` - `Pending` - `Degraded` - `Error` - `Offline` - `Failing` Bad labels: - `Green dot` - `Yellow circle` - `Red status` - `Status indicator` When the surrounding item already includes visible status text, the dot must stay decorative: ```html Active ``` When compact mode hides the visible text, the hidden label remains: ```html Active ``` --- ## Testing Strategy ### Automated / DOM Verification - Verify every rendered `StatusDot` has semantic text available either visibly or via `.sr-only`. - Verify the visual dot has `aria-hidden="true"` when a sibling label provides meaning. - Verify no implementation relies on `title` as the only accessible name. - Verify compact mode does not use `display:none`, `visibility:hidden`, or `aria-hidden="true"` on the status label. - Run axe-core against sidebar navigation, table rows, and card headers. ### Keyboard Testing - Tab through pages containing status dots. - Confirm dots are not separate tab stops. - Confirm parent links/buttons/rows remain keyboard accessible. ### Screen Reader Testing Test with NVDA, JAWS, and VoiceOver: - In full-label mode, user hears the item name and status meaning. - In compact mode, user still hears the status meaning. - User does not hear duplicate noise such as “green circle Active.” - User does not hear meaningless labels such as “red dot.” ### Visual Testing - Verify dot-to-background contrast is at least 3:1 in sidebar, table row, card header, hover, selected, light mode, dark mode, and high contrast mode. - Verify color is not the only available status cue because text exists visibly or for assistive technology. - Test Windows High Contrast / forced colors to confirm status meaning remains available. --- ## Implementation Tasks ### Task 1: Update `StatusDot` API and Semantics 🔍 **Review checkpoint after this task** **Files:** - `StatusDot` component file - Any shared status type/constants file - Component tests **Structure Stub:** ```html Status label ``` **ARIA Attributes:** - `aria-hidden="true"` on the visual dot because the sibling label provides the accessible meaning. WCAG: 1.1.1, 1.4.1. - No `title` as accessible name. WCAG: 4.1.2. - No `tabindex`. WCAG: 2.4.3. **Tests:** - Green renders semantic label such as `Active`. - Yellow renders semantic label such as `Warning`. - Red renders semantic label such as `Error`. - Visual dot is hidden from assistive tech. - Label remains present in compact mode. **WCAG Criteria:** - WCAG 1.1.1 Non-text Content - WCAG 1.3.1 Info and Relationships - WCAG 1.4.1 Use of Color - WCAG 4.1.2 Name, Role, Value ### Task 2: Apply Usage Rules Across Sidebar, Tables, and Cards 🔍 **Review checkpoint after this task** **Files:** - Sidebar item component - Table row component - Card header component **Requirements:** - Full-label mode may show visible text. - Compact mode must visually hide, not remove, the label. - Product-specific labels must match context: deployments may use `Passing/Degraded/Failing`; users may use `Online/Away/Offline`; tasks may use `Active/Pending/Blocked`. **Tests:** - Sidebar compact mode exposes status text to screen readers. - Table row status is announced with the row content. - Card header status is announced once, not duplicated. - No dot becomes focusable. ### Task 3: Verify Contrast and Forced Colors 🔍 **Review checkpoint after this task** **Requirements:** - Each dot variant meets 3:1 contrast against actual adjacent backgrounds. - If not, add a visible border/ring that meets 3:1 while preserving the required dot color. - Forced-colors mode preserves semantic meaning through text. **Tests:** - Dot contrast passes on sidebar, table, card, hover, selected, light, and dark backgrounds. - Windows High Contrast mode still conveys status meaning. --- ## a11y-Critic Review Checkpoints | Checkpoint | After Task | Focus Areas | |---|---|---| | 🔍 1 | Task 1 | Verify color is not sole status channel; dot is decorative; label is meaningful | | 🔍 2 | Task 2 | Verify compact mode keeps accessible text; no duplicate announcements | | 🔍 3 | Task 3 | Verify 3:1 non-text contrast and high contrast mode behavior | --- ## Failure Modes This Plan Prevents - Status conveyed only by green/yellow/red. - `aria-label="red dot"` instead of semantic status. - `title` used as the only accessible label. - Compact mode removing the label from assistive technology. - Dot announced redundantly before visible status text. - Contrast tested against white instead of actual adjacent backgrounds. - Static indicator incorrectly made focusable or interactive. ## References - [WAI-ARIA Authoring Practices Guide](https://www.w3.org/WAI/ARIA/apg/) - [WCAG 2.2 Quick Reference](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/quickref/) - WCAG 1.1.1 Non-text Content - WCAG 1.4.1 Use of Color - WCAG 1.4.11 Non-text Contrast - WCAG 4.1.2 Name, Role, Value
